codeSnapshot.label: correlation.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor

df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')
numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include='number')

# ── 1. Pearson correlation matrix ──
corr_pearson = numeric_cols.corr(method='pearson')

# ── 2. Spearman correlation (robust, monotone) ──
corr_spearman = numeric_cols.corr(method='spearman')

# ── 3. Heatmap visualization ──
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))
for ax, corr, title in zip(axes, [corr_pearson, corr_spearman], ['Pearson', 'Spearman']):
    mask = np.triu(np.ones_like(corr, dtype=bool))  # upper triangle
    sns.heatmap(corr, mask=mask, annot=True, fmt='.2f',
                cmap='coolwarm', vmin=-1, vmax=1, center=0, ax=ax)
    ax.set_title(f'{title} Correlation')
plt.tight_layout()

# ── 4. Significance test for each correlation ──
def corr_pvalue_matrix(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    cols = df.columns
    p_matrix = pd.DataFrame(index=cols, columns=cols, dtype=float)
    for c1 in cols:
        for c2 in cols:
            _, p = stats.pearsonr(df[c1].dropna(), df[c2].dropna())
            p_matrix.loc[c1, c2] = p
    return p_matrix

p_vals = corr_pvalue_matrix(numeric_cols)

# ── 5. Variance Inflation Factor (multicollinearity) ──
X = numeric_cols.drop(columns=['target']).dropna()
vif_data = pd.DataFrame({
    'feature': X.columns,
    'VIF': [variance_inflation_factor(X.values, i) for i in range(X.shape[1])]
}).sort_values('VIF', ascending=False)
print(vif_data)
# VIF > 10 → strong multicollinearity, consider removing or combining

eli3: Correlation measures how much two variables move together. If income and education both tend to be high or low for the same people, they are positively correlated. If one goes up while the other goes down, they are negatively correlated. If there's no systematic pattern, correlation is near zero.

The famous rule: correlation does not imply causation. Just because ice cream sales and drowning deaths both peak in summer doesn't mean ice cream causes drowning — a third variable (hot weather) drives both. Correlation is symmetric (X correlates with Y just as much as Y correlates with X), while causation has a direction.

In ML, correlation is used for two things: finding features that predict the target (high absolute correlation with target is good), and detecting multicollinearity (two features that correlate strongly with each other are redundant — keeping both confuses linear models).

whenWhy.title: Feature selection before model training
whenWhy.body: High absolute Pearson correlation between a feature and the target suggests the feature is likely predictive. This is a fast, univariate filter before more expensive feature selection methods. Caveat: zero correlation doesn't mean zero predictive power — a feature with non-linear or interaction effects can have near-zero marginal correlation but high conditional importance.

whenWhy.title: Diagnosing multicollinearity in linear models
whenWhy.body: In multiple linear regression, multicollinear predictors (absolute correlation above 0.9 between two features) cause inflated coefficient standard errors and near-singular design matrices. Individual coefficient estimates become highly sensitive to small data changes, making interpretation unreliable. The Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) quantifies this: VIF for feature j = 1 divided by (1 minus the R-squared from regressing feature j on all others). VIF above 10 (or above 5 using a conservative threshold) signals problematic multicollinearity.

whenWhy.title: EDA correlation heatmap
whenWhy.body: A Pearson correlation heatmap over all numeric features is a standard EDA output. It reveals: feature-target relationships (which features correlate with what you want to predict), feature-feature redundancy (candidates for removal or PCA), and data quality issues (a feature that correlates at |r| = 0.99 with the target in training data is a leakage red flag — check if it's derived from the target).

deepDiveIntro: The Pearson correlation coefficient is the covariance of X and Y divided by the product of their standard deviations. It measures the degree of linear association and always lies between -1 and 1: +1 is perfect positive linear association, -1 is perfect negative, and 0 is no linear association. Under the null hypothesis that the true correlation is zero, a standardised test statistic based on r follows a t-distribution with n-2 degrees of freedom, providing a formal test of significance. [1]

Spearman's rank correlation is the Pearson correlation of the ranks of the data rather than the raw values. It measures monotone (not necessarily linear) association. Kendall's tau counts the excess of concordant pairs (where both variables rank the same way) over discordant pairs, normalised by the total number of pairs. Kendall's tau is more robust to outliers and more appropriate for small samples with many ties. [2]

deepDive.heading: Pearson, Spearman, Kendall: Which to Use
deepDive.body: Pearson assumes both variables are continuous, ideally normally distributed, and the relationship is linear. It is sensitive to outliers. Use it when both variables are numeric, the relationship appears linear in a scatter plot, and there are no extreme outliers. Spearman is non-parametric and robust to outliers — use it when outliers are present, data is ordinal, or the relationship appears monotone but non-linear. Kendall's tau is non-parametric and based on concordant/discordant pairs; it is more interpretable (a value of 0.4 means 40% more concordant than discordant pairs) and better for small samples with many ties. For most ML feature analysis, Spearman is the practical default. [2]
deepDive.formula: Pearson r = Cov(X,Y)/(σ_X σ_Y);  Spearman ρ = 1 − 6Σdᵢ²/(n(n²−1))

deepDive.heading: Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)
deepDive.body: VIF for predictor j = 1 divided by (1 minus R-squared from regressing predictor j on all other predictors). A VIF of 1 means no multicollinearity. A VIF of 5 means predictor j can be explained by the others with R-squared 0.8. A VIF of 10 means R-squared 0.9 — 90% of predictor j's variance is explained by the others. VIF above 10 is conventionally considered problematic. Resolution: drop one of the correlated pair, combine them (PCA, sum), or use Ridge regression which shrinks collinear coefficients jointly. [1]

deepDive.heading: Spurious Correlation and Confounding
deepDive.body: Spurious correlation: two variables appear correlated due to a shared confounder (third variable). Per-capita cheese consumption correlates strongly with deaths by bedsheet tangling (Tyler Vigen, 2015) — both are driven by economic and temporal trends, not causality. In ML contexts, spurious correlations in training data become learned model associations that fail at deployment when the confounder is absent. Detection: check if the correlation persists when controlling for candidate confounders (partial correlation) or after stratifying by the suspected confounder. The correct remedy is understanding the data-generating process, not statistics alone. [1]

takeaways: Pearson r = Cov(X,Y)/(σ_X σ_Y) ∈ [−1,1]; measures linear association. t = r√(n−2)/√(1−r²) ~ t(n−2) for significance testing. [1]
Spearman ρ: rank-based, robust to outliers, measures monotone association. Kendall τ: concordant-discordant pair ratio, best for small samples with ties. [2]
VIF_j = 1/(1−R²_j): quantifies multicollinearity. VIF > 10 → problem; consider dropping, combining, or switching to Ridge regression. [1]
Correlation ≠ causation: a confounder drives both. Partial correlation (controlling for Z) tests whether X-Y correlation persists after removing Z's influence. [1]
High |r| with target at training time is a leakage red flag — verify the feature is computed purely from information available before the target event. [1]

edgeCases.q: How can two variables have zero Pearson correlation but be statistically dependent?
edgeCases.a: Pearson r only measures linear association. Example: X drawn from a symmetric distribution and Y = X squared. The covariance is zero by symmetry (positive and negative deviations cancel), so r = 0 — even though Y is a deterministic function of X. Spearman also fails here since the relationship is non-monotone. Detection: scatter plot immediately reveals the parabola. Statistical tests: Mutual Information captures any dependence (not just linear/monotone), Distance Correlation equals zero if and only if X and Y are independent. sklearn's mutual_info_regression estimates mutual information from data.

edgeCases.q: What is partial correlation and when should you use it?
edgeCases.a: Partial correlation between X and Y controlling for Z is the Pearson correlation between the residuals of regressing X on Z and the residuals of regressing Y on Z. It measures the association between X and Y after removing the linear effects of Z. The formula involves the pairwise correlations between all three variables. Use when you suspect a confounder Z is inflating the observed correlation between X and Y. Example: price and quality both increase with brand reputation (Z). Partial correlation removes the brand effect to reveal the direct price-quality relationship. In regression, the standardised coefficient for predictor j is equivalent to the partial correlation between predictor j and the outcome after removing all other predictors.

sources.label: Freedman, D., Pisani, R. & Purves, R. — Statistics, 4th Ed. (W.W. Norton, 2007)
Conover, W.J. — Practical Nonparametric Statistics, 3rd Ed. (Wiley, 1999)
